edges.description: GCNIS acquires i(12p) during progression to invasive tumor
Amplified KRAS on 12p contributes to signaling activation

nodes.description: Nearly all postpubertal TGCTs arise from germ cell neoplasia in situ (GCNIS, formerly carcinoma in situ or intratubular germ cell neoplasia unclassified). GCNIS cells resemble fetal gonocytes that failed to mature normally during embryonic development. The initiating malignant transformation most likely occurs in utero in primordial germ cells/gonocytes. GCNIS cells can remain dormant until puberty and then accumulate chromosomal abnormalities that drive malignant progression.

evidence.reference_title: RAS/Mitogen-Activated Protein Kinase Signaling Pathway in Testicular Germ Cell Tumors.
evidence.snippet: the genetic and epigenetic factors involved in the pathogenesis of testicular germ cell tumors (TGCTs)

evidence.reference_title: Recent Advancements in Research on DNA Methylation and Testicular Germ Cell Tumors: Unveiling the Intricate Relationship.
evidence.snippet: epigenetics has recently come into the spotlight as a major factor in TGCT initiation, progression, and even resistance to treatment

nodes.description: Isochromosome 12p [i(12p)] is the most consistent cytogenetic abnormality in TGCTs, present in more than 80% of cases. The remaining cases show 12p overrepresentation through other mechanisms. The short arm of chromosome 12 harbors genes including KRAS, CCND2 (cyclin D2), NANOG, and LDHB that drive tumor proliferation and maintain pluripotency.

evidence.reference_title: Epigenetic Regulation of Driver Genes in Testicular Tumorigenesis.
evidence.snippet: Epigenetic mechanisms, such as methylations of cytosines on the DNA string and methylations and acetylations of histone 3 lysines, regulate expression of these driver genes between the TGCT subtypes

nodes.description: Activating mutations in KIT and K-RAS are the most significantly mutated somatic genes in TGCTs. KIT mutations are found in approximately 18-25% of TGCTs, predominantly in seminomas, and activate the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT signaling pathways, promoting cell survival and proliferation.

evidence.reference_title: RAS/Mitogen-Activated Protein Kinase Signaling Pathway in Testicular Germ Cell Tumors.
evidence.snippet: several genomic and transcriptomic assays have identified a few significantly mutated somatic genes, primarily KIT and K-RAS
